name: Kerala Cold-Pressed Virgin Coconut Oil (500ml)
description: Cold-pressed from hand-picked coconuts grown on Kerala's Malabar Coast. Cold-pressing (below 49°C) preserves the lauric acid content at 52% — heat extraction destroys it. No chemicals, bleaching, or deodorisation. Pressed within 24 hours of cracking. Poured into amber glass bottles to prevent UV oxidation. The subtle sweet coconut fragrance indicates no heat damage. Used in Kerala households for cooking, hair oiling, and oil-pulling (kavala graha) — an Ayurvedic oral hygiene practice over 3,000 years old.

name: Uttarakhand Wild Himalayan Herb Tea Box (12 varieties)
description: A curated box of 12 Himalayan herbal teas hand-foraged above 2,500 metres in the Munsiyari and Milam glacier regions of Uttarakhand. Includes: Brahmi (memory and clarity), Ashwagandha root (adaptogen), Tulsi triple blend (immunity), Bhringraj (hair health), Kutki (liver support), Himalayan Nettle (iron-rich), Triphala (digestive), Jatamansi (sleep), Giloy (fever), Hadjod (bone), Wild Rose hip (Vitamin C), and Pushkarmool (respiratory). Each herb foraged, dried in shade, and packed by the 32-member women's collective.

name: Kumkumadi Tailam — Ayurvedic Face Oil (30ml)
description: Kumkumadi tailam is an Ayurvedic face oil prescribed in the Ashtanga Hridayam — the foundational Ayurvedic text written in the 7th century CE. This formulation uses 16 ingredients including saffron (kesar), Indian madder (manjistha), vetiver (ushira), red sandalwood (rakta chandana), and licorice (yashtimadhu) infused into sesame base oil over 72 hours of low-heat processing. Made by Vaidyasala Traditional Formulary in Kottakkal, certified by the Ministry of AYUSH. No synthetic fragrance, no preservatives, no fillers.

name: Sandalwood & Turmeric Ubtan Scrub (200g)
description: Traditional Ubtan body scrub using Karnataka sandalwood powder from government-licensed sustainable plantations in Mysuru, organic turmeric from Erode, chickpea flour, ground almonds, and rose water. Ubtan has been used as a bridal skin preparation ritual across South Asia for 3,000+ years. Used wet as a face and body scrub or dry as a face pack. The sandalwood is Karnataka Government Certified — wild sandalwood harvest is strictly controlled and only plantation-grown wood is legally available.

name: Shilajit Resin — Himalayan Grade A (20g)
description: Shilajit is a mineral resin that seeps from Himalayan rock crevices above 3,000 metres during the summer months, formed over millions of years from compressed plant matter. It contains over 85 ionic minerals including fulvic acid — a compound that significantly enhances mineral absorption in cells. This Grade A resin is collected by hand from Lahaul Spiti at 4,200 metres, sun-purified in the traditional method, and lab tested for 40+ heavy metals and contaminants. Fulvic acid content certified minimum 60%.

name: Cane & Jute Sling Bag — Manipuri
description: A structured sling bag combining a cane frame with hand-woven jute body panels. Made by members of Ima Keithel — the world's largest all-women market in Imphal, Manipur, operating continuously since 1533. The cane is soaked in natural water for 48 hours to make it pliable, then woven into a lattice frame. The jute is hand-woven on a traditional loin loom. Interior lining in organic cotton with one zip pocket. Naturally anti-bacterial, fully biodegradable, and load-bearing up to 8kg.

name: Dokra Brass Earrings — Tribal Motifs
description: Drop earrings cast in the Dokra lost-wax method by Rukhmani Dhurwa of Kondagaon, Bastar. The design features the Gond sun motif — eight rays radiating from a central disc, representing the eight directions of the Gondi cosmological map. Each pair is unique because the wax model is destroyed in the casting process. Weight: 18g per earring. The brass alloy used is recycled from old vessels collected from the community, making each pair a literal transformation of history.

name: Kantha Embroidered Tote Bag
description: A tote bag made from upcycled vintage cotton saris, repurposed and joined using the Kantha stitch — the running stitch technique originally used by Bengali women to stitch layers of worn saris into quilts and wraps, giving them new life. The Kantha work on this bag tells the story of the Padma River through waves, fish, boats, and water birds stitched in cotton thread. The handles are plaited from jute. Subhasree uses only thread dyed with natural indigo and madder. Load capacity: 12 kg.

name: Bidri Silver Inlay Cufflinks
description: Bidriware cufflinks using the 600-year-old technique developed under the Bahmani Sultanate. The base is cast from a zinc-copper alloy using soil from the Bidar fort walls — soil with a unique ammonium chloride content that permanently blackens the alloy surface. Pure silver wire is then hand-hammered into the engraved channels of the blackened surface. The contrast between matte black and bright silver is the defining aesthetic of Bidri work. Each pair takes 8 hours to complete.

name: Banjara Embroidery Coin Purse
description: A compact coin purse in the Banjara embroidery tradition of the Lambani nomadic tribal community of Karnataka. Banjara embroidery is distinguished by the combination of densely packed geometric stitchwork with tiny mirror discs, cowrie shells, and brass beads — all functional originally as talismans. The Banjara are India's most widespread nomadic group — their embroidery appears across six states under different names (Lambani in Karnataka, Banjara in Maharashtra, Sugali in Andhra). Thread dyed with pomegranate rind and henna.

name: Pattachitra Scroll — Jagannath Narrative
description: A 45-cm × 90-cm Pattachitra scroll painted by Gajendra Maharana of Raghurajpur — the heritage crafts village of Odisha where every household is an artist family. The scroll depicts the Dashavatara — the ten incarnations of Vishnu — in the traditional sequential narrative format. The canvas is made from layers of cotton cloth glued with tamarind paste, then coated with a mixture of chalk and tamarind. All colours are natural mineral and vegetable pigments: white from conch shell, black from lamp soot, yellow from stone harital, red from hingula (cinnabar), and green from leaves. The border pattern is the traditional beetle design.

name: Blue Pottery Vase — Jaipur
description: Jaipur Blue Pottery is unique among ceramics globally: it uses no clay. The body is made from a dough of quartz stone powder, powdered glass, Multani mitti (Fuller's earth), borax, and gum. This non-clay body makes it technically a fritware — a Central Asian tradition brought to Jaipur by the Mughals in the 14th century. The signature turquoise and cobalt blue designs are drawn freehand using oxide pigments before a single low-temperature firing at 800°C. This vase features the traditional flowering vine with mythical bird motifs from Mughal garden manuscripts.

name: Channapatna Lacquered Toy Set
description: A set of 6 Channapatna lacquered spinning tops and pull-along figures in ivory wood (soft ebony) from Channapatna — the 'Toy Town of India' — a tradition established by Tipu Sultan who invited Persian craftsmen in 1799 to teach the technique. Each toy is turned on a hand-powered lathe, then lacquered using natural lac (secreted by the Kerria lacca insect) applied while the toy is still spinning. The colours — red, yellow, and green — come from natural mineral pigments. BIS certified child-safe for ages 3 and above.

name: Kalamkari Pen-and-Dye Fabric Panel
description: A 60cm × 90cm cotton fabric panel in the Srikalahasti Kalamkari tradition — painted entirely by hand using a kalam (bamboo pen with pointed cloth wick). Unlike the block-printed Masulipatnam Kalamkari, Srikalahasti is drawn freehand without any stamps or stencils. This panel depicts a scene from the Ramayana — Rama crossing the ocean to Lanka — with 200 individual characters. All colours natural: fermented iron-rich mud (black), pomegranate rind (yellow), indigo (blue), madder (red). The fabric is washed in the Swarnamukhi river during processing — the minerals in the water fix the colours.

name: Kondapalli Wooden Dancing Doll
description: A Kondapalli dancing doll made from Tella poniki softwood (Wrightia tinctoria) — a wood so light it floats. Kondapalli toys are made in interlocking sections (head, torso, limbs) that are individually carved and then assembled with a tamarind paste adhesive. This creates the signature articulated movement — the doll's arms and head move independently when touched. Painted with natural zinc white as base, then decorated with mineral pigments. The craft is listed in the GI Registry and maintained by 500 families in Kondapalli village.
